My idea was to refactor the supported formats page since it currently fulfills a double purpose / has a bit of a split personality. It is a big list of all the format strings that exist and, once you know the correct string, you can navigate to the backend-specific docs to tell you the kwargs. 

The title hints at "here are all the formats you can use", which it does list, but if my desire is to "figure out which plugin I should use to read my _image.XYZ_ file" then I would indeed check the "Supported Formats", but the current list is a bit confusing. I would have expected to find a list or table that I can search (ctrl+F) for ".XYZ" and then it lists all the format strings (or backends) that I could use here.

Similarly, if I know that I want/need to use backend X (e.g. imageio-ffmpeg) then I need to _know_ that I need to search for this information in "Supported Formats" (and click on the correct format string in the list). Since this is something API-related, I would, however, have searched for this in the API reference.

Long story short, I would like to change "Supported Formats" into lists of the form

```
- <extension> [Format Full Name](link to description/wikipedia/standard)
    Backend Libraries: <list of format strings or backends>

- PNG [Portable Network Graphic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portable_Network_Graphics).
    Format String: :ref:`PNG-PIL <imageio.plugins.pillow>`, :ref:`PNG-FI <imageio.plugins.freeimage>`, ...
```

One list for _backends by format_ and one for _formats by backend_, or as a table (if I manage to figure out how to make a nice table in sphinx) so that we can maintain a single structure but index it by row or column for either of the two scenarios.

I would like to have this section here (Plugins & Backends) become the backend-specific docs, i.e., kwargs, recommended/supported versions, intricacies/gotchas, ...

Then again, I might be overthinking this and it is cleaner and easier to leave it as is?

The overall approach is good but a little more on the permissive side than the efficient side in terms of how it searches for functions.

1. When iterating over the module `body`, add a check so that we only recurse on `FunctionDef`s (with a matching name) and `ClassDef`s (to search for methods).
2. When iterative over a class def `body`, add a check so that we only recurse on `FunctionDef`s with a matching name.

In particular, you don't need to worry about finding `FunctionDef`s that are nested inside other types of syntax, e.g. if statements or even other `FunctionDef`s. It's fine to just look for function defs at the top-level of a module or class definition.

Originally I was getting the same problem described in boost::Hana. Dionne explains the following: "Here, we capture the enclosing args... by reference using [&], and we do not need to receive any arguments. Also, hana::eval_if assumes that its arguments are branches that can be called, and it will take care of calling the branch that is selected by the condition. However, this will still cause a compilation failure, because the bodies of the lambdas are not dependent anymore, and semantic analysis will be done for both branches even though only one would end up being used. The solution to this problem is to make the bodies of the lambdas artificially dependent on something, to prevent the compiler from being able to perform semantic analysis before the lambda is actually used. To make this possible, hana::eval_if will call the selected branch with an identity function (a function that returns its argument unchanged (_ function)), if the branch accepts such an argument". Now it's possible using the function overloading technique it's no longer required. I'll check Monday. I'll clarify though, the original implementation wasn't taken from boost. It's just I was seeing the same problems. Hence why I needed the _ identify function

  import tensorflow

I see you point. I looked deeper into the issue.

**Use functors instead of classes**: We are using policy classes like a functor. Maybe we should make policies functors? Then `stateful` can be a property. It would also fix another ugly flaw in the current policy implementation: The weight sharing between act and run models works solely based on the assumption that the same policy class is instantiated twice (once for run and once for learning) in the same `tf.variable_scope`. This is *very* brittle! 

**Examples**: What if we for some reason instantiate two different policies whose `tf.Variable` names overlap in the same scope? Suddenly they start sharing weights! 
What if the designer of an RL algorithm decides to put run and learn models in different scopes? Without looking deep into the tf graph she won't figure out why the run policy never improves.
What if there is one day a RL algorithm with dueling policies? We need to be aware that they need to be put in different variable scopes otherwise they start sharing weights and the duel is meaningless.

With a functor design we could ensure that weight sharing happens *iff* two models graphs are constructed by the same functor completely independent of any external variable scope.

What do you guys think? I am in favor of fixing the old underlying issues rather than adding more soso constructions to work around the underlying issues but I also understand that this involves a lot of change (possibly breaking backwards compatibility) and work.

`Digest`s are 40 bytes long, so you should never worry about holding them in memory.

A `Digest` is the in-memory representation of a snapshot stored in the LMDB database, so it does not force more than 40 bytes to be held in memory.

A `Snapshot` is slightly larger because it is a `Digest` + a list of filenames (ie, a bunch of strings), but still doesn't actually hold file content in memory.

So it seems to me we have a pattern of :

```python
if A:
  return B + (C * D)
else:
  return B + D
```

I think perhaps in this case, if we want to avoid the extra else perhaps we should extract more of the common part of the two expressions (e.g. the `if A` block should just scale the thing we add to `B`.

```python
if A:
  D = C * D
return B + D
```

Concretely in this case it would look like the following (I think we cannot use `*=` because this is not supported on `tf.Variable`):

```python
b = self.b
if multiplier is not None:
  b = b * multiplier
return inputs + b
```

The `MappingKernelManager` (well, actually, the previous `MultiKernelManager`) is responsible for equating a kernel manager instance with a kernel_id.  

The environment for which this value is derived is a portion of the kernel creation request that comes from clients of Enterprise Gateway.  In these applications, clients can influence the request.  It is not the environment of the process itself.  

As was noted in the next comment, by specifying things like kernel_id and kernel namespace, for example, clients can "seed" environments in which their kernels will be invoked.  Since kernel_id is a perfect "key", allowing clients to specify their kernel_id is critical to enabling them to build applications as they desire based on that value.

Please restore that functionality as it is current behavior.

> In other words, running `select 1 where 'a' > 'A'` and `select 1 where 'a' >= 'A'` both returns `1`. How would that solve @ricky26's issue?

For a _correctly_ configured database: `select 1 where 'a' > 'A'` should not return any results, whereas `select 1 where 'a' >= 'A'` should return `1` since `'a'` and `'A'` are considered equal when it comes to ordering in a case-insensitive database.

For an _incorrectly_ configured database: `select 1 where 'a' > 'A'` may return `1` (for example if it has case-sensitive ordering), and `select 1 where 'a' >= 'A'` can also return `1` for the same reason.

Since we only want to return results for incorrectly configured databases, we therefore use the first form (`>`) which does _not_ return `1` for a correctly configured database.

`self.range` is an alias for either `self.voltage_range` or self.current_range` depending on the mode. You might think that is it safe to replace a alias with a new definition of the parameter with the same command but it is not since the snapshot will be out of date e.g. if you set the voltage_range the snapshot of range will not update and the other way around. 

Futher to that there was an internal cache of the range value stored in _cache_range_value. That is unneeded and error prone since both `voltage_range` and `current_range` have their own internal cache and the alias to them called `range` therefor also does.

Rather than just creating an alias as the orginal code does it would be better to use a DelegateParameter https://qcodes.github.io/Qcodes/api/parameters/parameter.html?qcodes.instrument.parameter.DelegateParameter#qcodes.instrument.parameter.DelegateParameter for `range` and change its source as needed

To clarify, last sent is the record time of last uploaded, last check is the time we check if there's any cache need to be uploaded. So last check can be updated every 10 mins even there's no cache uploaded.

Think about this scenario if we use last check time to compare:
1. `07-14 15:00`, cx runs a cli command. A new record has been stored in cache file while the file size is 6k.
-  Since no telemetry uploaded, so last sent is the time of previous record, let's say it's `07-14 14:30`.
- Last check has been updated to `07-14 15:00`.
3. Then `07-15 14:40`, cs runs another command. Another record has been stored in cache file and the file size grows to 9k.
-  now(`07-15 14:40`) - last check time(`07-14 15:00`) is less than 24 hours, so cache file won't be uploaded
- last sent time is still `07-14 14:30`, last check time is updated to `07-15 14:40`
5. Another 10 mins later, `07-15 14:50`, cs runs another command. Another record has been stored in cache file and the file size grows to 12k.
- now(`07-15 14:50`) - last check time(`07-15 14:40`) is less than 24 hours, so cache file won't be uploaded
- last sent time is still `07-14 14:30`, last check time is updated to `07-15 14:50`
......
......
If customer keeps running cli commands, last check time will keep updating, cache file will never be uploaded until rotated with cache.1

- How is my data split up? Do older records need updating? - partitions

- Do I want it to run every time a new file is added or update data in batches? - cursor sensors

What you proposed is different to mine. This way it will work. The difference is that it must proceed to the second try-else block after the `isinstance(instance, DeclarativeBase)` returns false. In this case we save time by skipping importing `DeclarativeMeta` if a model is defined the new way.

```        try:
            from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase  # type: ignore
        except ImportError:
            pass
        else:
            if isinstance(instance, DeclarativeBase):
                return True

        try:
            from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import DeclarativeMeta  # type: ignore
        except ImportError:
            pass
        else:
            return isinstance(instance.__class__, DeclarativeMeta)```

so i took a look at current usages of `rev` in `.pre-commit-config.yaml`-files (via github api [`https://api.github.com/search/code?q=filename:%2Epre-commit-config%2Eyaml&page={page}`]) and extracted all revs field. here's what i got so far:

- we can detect shas in general with `r"^[a-z0-9]+$"`, as they all only contain **lowercase** letters and numbers. this will result in false positives for branches, tho.
- version tags are all messed up. most of them follow the pattern `r"^v?([0-9]+\.)+[0-9]$"`, but:
  - some (e.g. ["20.8b1"](https://github.com/psf/black/releases/tag/20.8b1)) contain letters
  - some (e.g. ["4.3.21-2"](https://github.com/PyCQA/isort/releases/tag/4.3.21-2)) contain dashes
  thus, the only reasonable way to detect tags - in my opinion - is, to whitelist all strings that contain dots. (e.g contains `r"[^\.]\.[^\.]"`)
- negative examples i found are:
  - `"stable"`
  - `"master"`
  - `""`

thus, this is my suggestion (tested it against the data i got from the api calls):
```python
bad_revs = {
    "stable",
    "master",
    "HEAD",
}
rev = dct.get(self.key, "")

if "." not in rev and (
    not re.match(r"^[a-z0-9]+$", rev)
    or any(bad_rev in rev for bad_rev in bad_revs)
    or not rev
):
    print(f"Warning: {rev}")
```

Yes from the view of the `rollout_worker` the behavior shouldn't change.
The difference is that iterating over `items()` will access both the key and the policy, i.e. internally `PolicyMap` will call `__getitem__`. `__getitem__` will access the disk, if the selected policy is not in the cache. Every policy is accessed and then *afterwards* the if statement in the next line checks whether we are actually interested in this policy. With my changes we only call `__getitem__` for those policies that pass through the if statement. This way we reduce the number of unnecessary access of policies and in turn reduce the number of unnecessary `_read_from_disk()` (Compare `rllib/policy/policy_map.py`).
The same goes for `get_weights()`.

In short: Don't do `policy_map[pid]` if we skip this `pid` anyway, due to the if statement.

Thanks @wkcn :). Basically `PackedFunc` does not go across the dll boundary. It remains in the MXNet side.

When application A calls PackedFunc, it first passes the function name to MXNet. Then MXNet finds the corresponding PackedFunc, and returns the address of the PackedFunc back to A (A may cache the name-address map). Finally, A invokes that PackedFunc with the address.

To conclude, A is not aware of std::function. It only gets an address or some other unique identifier of PackedFunc. And the address is just a void*, which is ABI compatible.

In postgres, `select count(*)` has basically the same performance as `select *` (it loads each row to check its visibility in the current transaction).  The count operation is `select count(*) from azure_queue_messages msgs where msgs.queue_name = azure_queue_count.queue_name`, the where clause for which is a prefix of this index, so it will use the index to scan the rows for that queue name.

I understand the goal is to ensure the ICMP6 filter is applied to the message that are passed from user/host to Thread (which I think is the original goal of this `if` block).

But the earlier changing of `aFromNcpHost` behavior and removing of the check here has a side-effect that now the same filter will also be applied to messages received by device (this code path is used for handling all messages whether received by device over Thread radio or given by user).

In terms of ICMP6:
- Within OT core we process echo request/response and `AddressResolver` processes `kTypeDstUnreach`) which are included in `sForwardICMPTypes` (so they are allowed and not filtered) 
- But we also have public OT ICMP6 API `otIcmp6RegisterHandler` which allows a user of OT to register a specific handler to process received ICMP6 message. So the filtering can now potentially apply the message types that can be used here as well.

I think the change in this PR in fine and make sense to filter in all cases. But just wanted to point this out so that we are aware of this change in behavior (in case it comes up later).

> how do you want the production jobs to work? I.e. do you really want the data quality checks to run in a job that's independent from the job that refreshes the asset? Would you not want to re-run the data quality checks each time the asset is re-generated?

Excellent questions... and currently I'm unsure.  Let's discuss.

Philosophically I think that a monitoring system should be in a different failure domain to the system being monitored - ie; I don't want the monitoring to fail for the same reason that the thing being monitored fails.

For example; should the "scheduling system" fail to trigger an update of the data asset I want the monitoring to start failing because the asset has stale data.

I think this requires the data quality checks to run in an independent job (possibly running in an independent Dagster deployment?)

I don't see any harm in *also* running the data quality checks each time the asset is re-generated; since this would provide the quickest possible detection of a problem.  However, I don't think this is the only place they can run since it seems that there would be many failure cases that would impact both the data generating job and the data monitoring job.

We don't need the child pull request in order to find the parent pull request. We can do it from the parent/child branch naming convention:

1. If branch_name doesn't have a double underscore, we can exit early (it's not a child)
2. Split branch_name on double underscore and take the first part as the parent branch name (i.e. starting with feature/parent__child we expect the parent branch name to be feature/parent)
3. Look for a pull request where head = the parent branch name and base is repo.default_branch. This should be the parent pull request.

Ok, I read it.. and have few questions. From points 8 and 9 of the specification:

> 8. Breaking changes MUST be indicated at the very beginning of the footer or body section of a commit. A breaking change MUST consist of the uppercase text BREAKING CHANGE, followed by a colon and a space.
> 9. A description MUST be provided after the BREAKING CHANGE:, describing what has changed about the API, e.g., BREAKING CHANGE: environment variables now take precedence over config files.

So why did you choose so complicated approach?
What you can do is simply match the beginning of the body/footer. Then parse breaking change description without removing it from the sections. It's still part of them. So for example:
```python
if body:
    body = body[2:]
    breaking_change = extract_breaking_change(body)  # returns breaking description
if footer:
    footer = footer[2:]
    breaking_change = extract_breaking_change(footer)
```
Now we have a few points for discussion:
- How will we deal with cases when both, body and footer will contain BREAKING CHANGE?
- How will we deal with cases when multiple BREAKING CHANGE comments will be in one section?

No, it's important to call `apply` function, because otherwise we can't retrieve `dtypes` from 'by' columns:

```python
# When we do:
self._apply_agg_function(lambda df: df.dtypes)
# That means that inside `groupby_agg_builder` we will apply 
# passed lambda to our pandas groupby object:
df.groupby(by, **groupby_kwargs).dtypes
```
And because we're always set `as_index=True` to track metadata, we will always get mismatched 'by' columns, even if the original `as_index` value was `False`:
```python
>>> pd_df
  col0  col1  col2 col3
0    a     1     5  one
1    b     2     6  one
2    a     3     7  two
3    a     4     8  two
>>> pd_df.groupby(["col0", "col3"], as_index=False).dtypes  # That's the result we want
     col0   col1   col2    col3
0  object  int64  int64  object
1  object  int64  int64  object
2  object  int64  int64  object
>>> pd_df.groupby(["col0", "col3"], as_index=True).dtypes  # That's the result we'll get.
            col1   col2					   # As we can see, there is no way
col0 col3                                                  # to restore 'col0' and 'col3' dtypes values
a    one   int64  int64
     two   int64  int64
b    one   int64  int64
```
So the solution here is to use `apply` function, that does not drop 'by' columns from frame:
```python
>>> pd_df.groupby(["col0", "col3"], as_index=True).apply(lambda df: df.dtypes)
             col0   col1   col2    col3
col0 col3
a    one   object  int64  int64  object
     two   object  int64  int64  object
b    one   object  int64  int64  object

# So what we want to do here is:
self._apply_agg_function(lambda grp: grp.apply(lambda df: df.dtypes))
# Which is the same as:
self.apply(lambda df: df.dtypes)
```
Note: `drop` parameter, that indicate whether we should drop 'by' column before aggregation or not, works only in case of single-column groupby: https://github.com/modin-project/modin/blob/e5556b5c531b52546a76690bc976aa90995862a7/modin/pandas/groupby.py#L879-L885

> This PR will always try to add subnet `10.0.0.0/24` to the vnet. It'll succeed only if `10.0.0.0/24` is valid within the vnet, which is very unlikely in most cases. In other cases:
> 
>     1. If the existing vnet `10.0.0.0/16` already has subnet `10.0.0.0/24`, the creation will fail because the space is already occupied
> 
>     2. If the existing vnet is `10.3.0.0/16`, the creation will fail because the prefix is not in the allowed address space:
>        > Azure Error: NetcfgInvalidSubnet
>        > Message: Subnet 'vm3Subnet' is not valid in virtual network 'vnet3'.
> 
>     3. If the existing vnet's address space is the same as the existing subnet's prefix - both `10.0.0.0/16`, the subnet creation will fail in any case, because there is no space left
> 
>     4. If the existing vnet's address space is `10.0.0.0/24`, how do we decide the mask of the subnet? 24, 25? It is not practical to do the decision for the user
> 
> 
> **My suggestion**: If `--vnet-name` and `--subnet` are neither specified, `az create` will detect if there is already a vnet and subnet in the resource group and put the vm there. We can apply the same when only `--vnet-name` is specified with an existing vnet - if there is a subnet, put the vm there; if not, error out, instead of calculating the available prefix and creating a subnet voluntarily.

def git_remote(git_repo):
    """Return the URL for remote git repository.

    Depending on the system setup it returns ssh or https remote.
    """
    # if not matching something/something
    # such as a local directory ".", then
    # simply return this unmodified.
    if not re.match(r'^[^/]+/[^/]+$', git_repo):
        return git_repo

    github_token = os.getenv(GITHUB_TOKEN_KEY)
    if github_token:
        return f'https://{github_token}@github.com/{git_repo}'
    return f'git@github.com:{git_repo}'
```

It's considered bad form to put in a mutable data structure as the default argument to a function, because it is instantiated only once. If someone changes the code so that the mutable is changed (e.g. `func_kwargs.update(...)`), it gets modified for every subsequent call to that function. See e.g. [here](https://docs.quantifiedcode.com/python-anti-patterns/correctness/mutable_default_value_as_argument.html) and [here](https://florimond.dev/blog/articles/2018/08/python-mutable-defaults-are-the-source-of-all-evil/), but Google provides a vast treasure trove of people feeling the pain here.

Sure, I should have said that I don't think copying would usually be the **bottleneck** in Python. And I don't think the small cost of copy outweighs the clarity benefits of immutability. Basically, I think it's a premature optimization. 

The numpy binding does get a speed benefit from the fact that we don't copy the memory from C, and instead re-uses it in the numpy array or memory view.

In the numpy case, however, I can't imagine a use case where someone would want to work on an array of H3 indexes **in place**. That sort of thing makes sense in something like a linear algebra library, but I'm not sure I see it for a fixed-length set of hexagons.

I'm open to adding it as an option, but I would love to see a use case for it first. 

That said, for this first release, I think we need to keep `set` and `list` instead of their immutable counterparts, just so we match the current API.

Thanks @calvernaz ! This change effectively causes the region to be returned from `$AWS_DEFAULT_REGION` (an AWS env config), as opposed to the `$DEFAULT_REGION` (our (deprecated) env config). In fact, due to a recent change where we introduced multi-region support for Community, the whole concept of "local regions" or "default regions" is actually deprecated/obsolete. (In other words, at some point we should probably get rid of this entire `get_local_region(..)` function, or at least not depend on it, only as a fallback if no other region can be determined.)

I think the more general issue here is that, for an incoming API Gateway request to, say `apiId123`, we need to identify the target region first. We used to use a cache (lookup table) in `API_REGIONS` (populated upon creation of an API), but this seems like a brittle approach. The more robust approach would be to simply loop through all regions and APIs to find and look up the API with ID `apiId123` (probably acceptable from a performance PoV, given that number of API Gateways is usually low).

Thoughts?

    def _ir_wv_counts_to_radiance(self, counts, channel):
        """Convert IR/WV counts to radiance.

        Reference: [PUG], equations (4.1) and (4.2).
        """
        a, b = self._get_calibration_keys('radiance')
        rad = a + b * counts
        return rad.where(rad > 0, np.float32(np.nan))

    def _get_calibration_keys(key_type):
        nc_key_a = self.nc_keys_coefs[channel][key_type]['a']
        nc_key_b = self.nc_keys_coefs[channel][key_type]['b']
        a = np.float32(self.nc[nc_key_a])
        b = np.float32(self.nc[nc_key_b])
        return a, b

    def _ir_wv_radiance_to_brightness_temperature(self, rad, channel):
        """Convert IR/WV radiance to brightness temperature.

        Reference: [PUG], equations (5.1) and (5.2).
        """
        a, b = self._get_calibration_keys('brightness_temperature')
        bt = b / (np.log(rad) - a)
        return bt.where(bt > 0, np.float32(np.nan))

Luckily the other work has already been done (see the link in https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/issues/28389#issuecomment-1129221519). Your proposal can be adapted into the `_quality_args()` method from yt-dlp, 
```py
    def _quality_args(self, codec):
        if self._preferredquality is None:
            return []
        elif self._preferredquality > 10:
            return ['-b:a', self._preferredquality + 'k']

        limits = {
            'libmp3lame': (10, 0),
            'libvorbis': (0, 10),
            # FFmpeg's AAC encoder does not have an upper limit for the value of -q:a.
            # Experimentally, with values over 4, bitrate changes were minimal or non-existent
            'aac': (0.1, 4),
            'libfdk_aac': (1, 5),
            'lib_opus': (.33, 10),
        }.get(codec)
        if not limits:
            return []
        q = limits[1] + (limits[0] - limits[1]) * (self._preferredquality / 10)
        if codec == 'libfdk_aac':
            return ['-vbr', compat_str(int(q))]

        elif codec == 'lib_opus':
            # The opus codec doesn't support the -q:a option
            # Give quality numbers 0-10 *some* meaning (as suggested by "knarrff" on Github)
            # 0 (best quality) gives 192 kb/s
            # 5 (default) gives 99 kb/s
            # 10 (worst quality) gives 6 kb/s, which is the opus minimum 
            return ['-b:a', '%.0fk' % (19.2 * q, )]
return ['-q:a', compat_str(q)]
```
This gives 99k instead of 96k for the default and 192k for the best; according to the HydrogenAudio page linked by @knarff anything over 160k is going to be better than almost all the original sources that yt-dl sees. Rather, `Field`s always have default values embedded in their `.value` implementations because the `raw_value` in their constructor may always be `None`, which represents the user not specifying that field explicitly in the BUILD file:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/9259097a86a6ec7294f280a22d2b91053d1b9d31/src/python/pants/engine/target.py#L426-L433

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/9259097a86a6ec7294f280a22d2b91053d1b9d31/src/python/pants/backend/python/rules/targets.py#L120-L131

Sometimes, that default is as simple as `None`:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/9259097a86a6ec7294f280a22d2b91053d1b9d31/src/python/pants/engine/target.py#L436-L451

--

So, to avoid duplication of having to define "what does default mean" in two places, we would instead do this:

```
def maybe_get(self, field: Type[_F]) -> _F:
    result = ...
    if result is None:
       return field(raw_value=None, address=self.address)
```

I don't think `maybe_get` is very descriptive, at that point. Instead, I think it would be `Target.get_or_default()`?

What do you think about `Target.get_or_default()`? I really like that! I think it's better than `maybe_get()`. It avoids Stu's concern from this morning of downstream rules having an explosion of how to express if a target has a field

* field is simply not registered (i.e. `Target.maybe_get()`)
* field is registered, but not specified in the BUILD file (i.e. `Field(raw_value=None)`)
* field is registered and specified in the BUILD file (e.g. `Field(raw_value=True)`)

If we don't have this check, then C will see that B is up and has higher priority and so it will also not race for leadership. Thus, we'll end up in a scenario where no one goes for leadership even though both B and C have positive `failover_priority` (which we want to mean that

I think reliability is more important than keeping the existing behavior, especially if the new behavior is identical to the existing behavior when everything works fine. Only when things don't work fine immediately (Connection Refused, Service Unavailable, Too Early, Too Many Requests) we retry, which is exactly what we should be doing (knowing what we know now).

The reason for choosing 4 retries with a backoff of 2 seconds means we will retry 4 times no longer than 30 seconds. Which is exactly the same as we have specified for a connection timeout. So I think this acceptable, if you accept that an unresponsive system will also take 30 seconds by default before it fails.

Not retrying in the above cases is the wrong behavior IMO.

Generally, nested `parallel_for` invocations is almost a guaranteed deadlock. Do you not observe this in your testing? My experience has been that the outer invocation will fill the entire global thread pool with instances of its lambda, so that all n threads are inside this call:
https://github.com/apple/turicreate/blob/38c18e019be5b0c202790bedd6113d13d5d59796/src/core/parallel/lambda_omp.hpp#L115
But then that lambda will itself call `parallel_for`, submitting the inner lambda do the pool and waiting here:
https://github.com/apple/turicreate/blob/38c18e019be5b0c202790bedd6113d13d5d59796/src/core/parallel/lambda_omp.hpp#L120
But the join never finishes because the inner lambdas never run because all n threads are still waiting for the outer lambdas to finish.

Either way, the verifier needs to know the hashes of blocks _b_i_ to verify the proof.  However, the verifier must also verify that:

1. these blocks exist
2. these blocks are in the same order on the burn chain
3. these blocks belong to the same fork

The verifier _could_ verify a proof without the prover having to construct shunt proofs by building and maintaining its own fork table.  To do so, the verifier would need to _a priori_ fetch the burn chain and the Stacks chain headers.  Then, all the prover would need to supply are indexes _i_ to the blocks to which the intermediate nodes' backpointers were followed, and the verifier could fill in the rest.

Unfortunately, having verifiers fetch both chain headers is a tall order for low-power verifiers, like those within a Web browser or on a mobile phone.  The advantage of proving both (1) and (2) in your question is that it removes the need for the verifier to build its own fork table.  Instead, it can use a commodity burn chain block explorer to spot-check that Stacks block headers _b_i_ correspond to sortition transactions in the burn chain, and are the given number of blocks apart.

Since you have branched from `feature/language-registration` you don't have to use `--onto`. `--onto` is used only if you are rebasing on some other branch. See `man git-rebase` for some nice examples.

All commits from your branch are applied on the top of the base branch one by one. It's like you just did those changes/commits manually. Some of them might be in conflict with the changes in the base branch in which case you should resolve the conflicts (see `git status` and search for conflict markers in files). Once resolved you continue the rebase with `git rebase --continue`). At any time  you can abort the rebase and return to the previous state with `git rebase --abort`). If you run rebase in interactive mode you get a chance to squash, reword, split, augment etc. each commit by giving a special commands in the editor. That is a nice tool when you are need to tidy up the branch.

Tip: git keeps track of all branch positions (branches are like pointers) so if you understand this mechanism you can dissect and change your branch history with confidence as all commits you have made are there until garbage collecting kicks in when all dangling commits (without pointers -- refs: branches, tags pointing to them) are removed. To see this log of where your branch pointer was in the past see `git reflog`. And `git reset` can be used to move your branch pointer to any point in history. Of course, these operations should be used rarely and never on the published branch (e.g. never on `master`) as the published history should be immutable.

So I think the reason this was failing is that in graph mode, when it compiles the TensorFlow code into a graph, all of the branches of the `tf.cond()`s are inserted as parts of the graph (even if those branches won't be taken when the graph is actually executed).  When doing that, TensorFlow does shape inference, described a little bit here: https://www.tensorflow.org/guide/create_op#shape_functions_in_c

Basically, it tries to compute what the resulting shape will be for all of the ops in all of the branches.  At the line where we currently do `tf.fill([bucket_count - 1], 0)`, because `bucket_count` is a constant, it is able to determine at compile time that this will result in `tf.fill([-1], 0)` and the shape function for `tf.fill` emits an error, because this would result in an output shape that doesn't make sense.

Hence the error (at least, the one that I was seeing when trying this PR locally):

> ValueError: Fill dimensions must be >= 0 for '{{node zero_bucket_count/write_summary/buckets/cond/cond/Fill_1}} = Fill[T=DT_INT32, index_type=DT_INT32](zero_bucket_count/write_summary/buckets/cond/cond/Fill_1/dims, zero_bucket_count/write_summary/buckets/cond/cond/Fill_1/value)' with input shapes: [1], [] and with input tensors computed as partial shapes: input[0] = [?].

In other words, it doesn't actually mean it's picking the wrong branch of the conditional to execute; it's that during graph compilation, it visits all the branches regardless of what will be ultimately executed.

(Aside: you could argue that when `bucket_count` is a constant 0, we could actually optimize away all the `tf.cond` ops and all the branches besides the empty case, at which point we wouldn't hit this error.  I'm guessing that the compilation's constant-folding isn't quite smart enough to do that.  Also, if you change the test to pass `bucket_count=tf.Variable(0)` and replace the use of `max()` with `tf.math.maximum()` as mentioned in the other comment, it will also pass, since now that the bucket count is a variable, it doesn't do as much shape inference and can't raise the false positive error.)

Ultimately, the best way to fix this is to just ensure that all the branches are still creating the correctly shaped tensor of dimensions `(bucket_count, 3)`, even when we know that branch will never be executed. Here's a patch that gets the test to pass:

```diff
diff --git i/tensorboard/plugins/histogram/summary_v2.py w/tensorboard/plugins/histogram/summary_v2.py
index bff980492..e1cd01846 100644
--- i/tensorboard/plugins/histogram/summary_v2.py
+++ w/tensorboard/plugins/histogram/summary_v2.py
@@ -425,6 +425,8 @@ def _buckets_v3(data, bucket_count=None):
     with tf.name_scope("buckets"):
         tf.debugging.assert_scalar(bucket_count)
         tf.debugging.assert_type(bucket_count, tf.int32)
+        # Treat a negative bucket count as zero.
+        bucket_count = tf.math.maximum(0, bucket_count)
         data = tf.reshape(data, shape=[-1])  # flatten
         data = tf.cast(data, tf.float64)
         data_size = tf.size(input=data)
@@ -440,7 +442,7 @@ def _buckets_v3(data, bucket_count=None):
             2. If the input data is empty, a tensor of shape (bucket_count, 3)
               of all zero values will be returned.
             """
-            return tf.zeros((max(0, bucket_count), 3), dtype=tf.float64)
+            return tf.zeros((bucket_count, 3), dtype=tf.float64)

         def when_nonempty():
             min_ = tf.reduce_min(input_tensor=data)
@@ -480,11 +482,12 @@ def _buckets_v3(data, bucket_count=None):
                 """When input data contains a single unique value."""
                 # Left and right edges are the same for single value input.
                 edges = tf.fill([bucket_count], max_)
-                # Counts for the first {bucket_count - 1} buckets [v, v) are 0.
-                zero_bucket_counts = tf.fill([bucket_count - 1], 0)
-                # Count for last bucket [v, v] is {data_size}.
+                # Bucket counts are 0 except the last bucket (if bucket_count > 0),
+                # which is `data_size`. Ensure that the resulting counts vector has
+                # length `bucket_count` always, including the bucket_count==0 case.
+                zeroes = tf.fill([bucket_count], 0)
                 bucket_counts = tf.cast(
-                    tf.concat([zero_bucket_counts, [data_size]], 0),
+                    tf.concat([zeroes[:-1], [data_size]], 0)[:bucket_count],
                     dtype=tf.float64,
                 )
                 return tf.transpose(a=tf.stack([edges, edges, bucket_counts]))
```

Yeesh, I was surprised to find that Python's docs for this are astoundingly awful, but passing `0` here should have the server select a port, which allows you to remove `get_free_port` and a possible race condition.

Here's an example with `ThreadedTCPServer`:

``` python
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Port 0 means to select an arbitrary unused port
    HOST, PORT = "localhost", 0

    server = ThreadedTCPServer((HOST, PORT), ThreadedTCPRequestHandler)
    ip, port = server.server_address

    # Start a thread with the server -- that thread will then start one
    # more thread for each request
    server_thread = threading.Thread(target=server.serve_forever)
    # Exit the server thread when the main thread terminates
    server_thread.daemon = True
    server_thread.start()
    print "Server loop running in thread:", server_thread.name

    client(ip, port, "Hello World 1")
    client(ip, port, "Hello World 2")
    client(ip, port, "Hello World 3")

    server.shutdown()
    server.server_close()
```

Here you're returning the job's name as the first item, where you need to be returning the job's ID number (an int).

```
jobID = int(job.metadata.name[len(self.jobPrefix):])
```

You also have this big long tuple of things that reach deep into `job` repeated twice. You probably want to condense this down:

```
jobID = int(job.metadata.name[len(self.jobPrefix):])
terminated = job.status.container_statuses[0].state.terminated
runtime = (termianted.finished_at - terminated.started_at).total_seconds()
result = (jobID, terminated.exit_code, runtime)
```

And then use `result` instead of the giant expression in both places.

You also need to make sure to [delete the job that has finished, and wait for it to no longer exist](https://github.com/DataBiosphere/toil/pull/2848/files#diff-183d11f3af830162fadf362f8c641fdfR631-R650), before returning. Otherwise, the next time someone asks for an updated job, that job we already reported (when we saw it finish) will still be there in Kubernetes in the finished state, so we'll grab it and report it as having finished again, which we aren't supposed to do.

Besides "*" we also support "," as key/label filter. For example, if I want to list items with two labels, I can do --label "labelA,labelB". For null label items, we used to use empty string to represent it. But it breaks if we want to list items with labelA and null label.  --label ",labelA" will be treated as just labelA in our service backend. The reason for using "\0" is that it is the escape for null in python. There is a principle that we are trying to follow. CLI won't interpret inputs for key/label filters. Thus, by using "\0" CLI will just blindly get the input arguments, unescape, url encode and send out request. When backend service gets the value, it will map to null and labelA.

Ideally, `Convert_` would
- leave `True`, `False` and valid `int`s (i.e., `0 < abs(x) <= self.m`) untouched,
- convert registered names into the corresponding valid values,
- error out in case of invalid inputs.

Before this change, it would silently accept wrong inputs and return them as-is which would likely lead to raising some unexpected errors shortly after.
After this change, it silently accepts wrong inputs and adds corresponding new variables for non-mapped names.

Both behaviors, before and after, are wrong, so this change didn't make things much worse ;). It might make things fail more silently, though.

Note: here we allow any patch version, but in `ci.sh` we determined we must specify the patch version. 

This was discovered while working on https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/7235. There, we have system Python 2.7 already, but must install a new version of Python 2.7 to get different unicode settings (UCS2 vs UCS4). We were running into issues with the interpreter changing between what was used to run Pants and what we used for subprocesses.

Should we default to constraining to the exact patch version too? Because this constraint is determined dynamically at runtime, I would advocate yes. The dynamic determination means that my computer could be using 3.7.1 and another's could be using 3.7.2, for example, without any issues. All it ensures is that that Pants run uses the exact same interpreter for subprocesses that it used for $PY / the virtualenv.

So, in order to support `.Values.global.serviceAccountName` with a default to `.Values.serviceAccount.name`, I rewrote this whole stanza.

1. `.Values.global.serviceAccountName`
2. `.Values.serviceAccount.name`
3. `printf "%s-user-deployments"`

`az debug echo` is designed to overcome the limitations of `--debug`:

- `az --debug '{"a":"b"}'` fails with a parser error and shows many irrelevant lines. `az debug echo` will succeed and only show the argument value.
- `az --debug @test.json` can't show the file content of the argument (only shows `'@test.json'`). `az debug echo` shows the actual file content.

`az debug echo` is hidden (not user-visible).

This is side-effecting, which makes it a vaguely sketchy thing to do in a `@rule` rather than at the top level in the `@goal_rule` if there is any chance that the `Process` is not idempotent. For example: this process invoke will be cached (unless you pass a non-default `CacheScope` for the `*Process`), and will not re-run after succeeding. By default, failed processes are not cached, so that part should be fine. But it's also the case that while executing a cache lookup the `N+1th` time this process runs, we might retry the process and then cancel it.

So. An important question for this `Goal`'s API is: do you think that it is a reasonable requirement that all publishing commands/destinations MUST be idempotent? _If so_, then I think that the current API works. _If not so_, then rather than actually executing the publish processes in `@rule`s, you would want to return them as a list of publish `InteractiveProcess`es to have the `@goal_rule` execute synchronously in the foreground.

In my experience, idempotent publish commands are generally a good idea, but it's something to be cautious of here.

Yes, 256 binary characters, which we wouldn't want to stuff in to a django `CharField`! 

Part of the problem is Django sets its own limits when dealing with mysql; it has no idea (and no way to specify) what the content you are storing in a CharField is, and so if you are using mysql [it sets the limit](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.2/ref/databases/#notes-on-specific-fields) on the maximum length of a `CharField` to be 255, and doesn't support db indexes on `TextField`. Django only provides one field for storing binary data, `BinaryField`, which on mysql maps to `LONGBLOB`; this suffers from the same issue that we have with `TEXT`, mysql requires a maximum key length for an index, and Django provides no way for us to specify this.

`jti` is designed for specifically for identifying a JWT; we have a JWT that is signed by us, we've verified the signature, `exp` and `nbf`, we now just want to know if it is still valid in the database.

I'm not even sure we should be storing the contents of the ID token. We use the ID token model for:

* Checking to see if the token exists in our database and hasn't been revoked
* Determining the application it was issued to
* Determining the scopes it was issued for

None of this requires storing the actual token itself if we are indexing by the `jti`. If access to the claims is also required; well we've received the JWT and validated it, so the claims are available directly in the JWT.

This is also desirable from a PII point of view; if we don't store the contents of the ID token, then there is no PII at all in the `IDToken` table.

Function CurveItemWithPOI.\_\_becameVisible refactored with the following changes:
- Simplify logical expression

> but the regex as it is does not match without them

There's a difference between `re.match` and `re.search`:
- https://docs.python.org/3/library/re.html#re.Pattern.match
- https://docs.python.org/3/library/re.html#re.Pattern.search

By wrapping the entire regex in `.*`, you're implicitly turning every `match` call into a `search` call.

The greedy qualifier `*?` is also not needed and maybe even bad, because the site could add another script tag with JSON data and break your implementation with that. Unless the JSON data doesn't contain a `</script>` tag, the greedy qualifier is not needed.

> Do action support forking, i.e., could we fork the action in your repo to be associated with the ipwb repo then merge from upstream later if need be to keep the ipwb action in check?

Actions are nothing but repositories with special metadata and code, so yes, one can fork them like any other repository. In fact, the most compelling part of this PR is the last segment that is forked from another account into my account. So, now the state of this PR is basically all the code is either coming from the GH's official action repositories that are unlikely to go rogue uncaught and remaining are coming from my account (if I am trusted enough), no other third-party accounts.

That said, `ipwb` is not an account, it is a repository, so there is no concept of forking a repo into another repo as such. You can fork the repo in, say, `oduwsdl` scope and reference from there, but then that means there will be many points of failures. Currently, if my account gets compromised, things may go wrong, but in that case any account with sufficient access to the `oduwsdl` organization can play nasty.

We can vendor actions locally in this repo itself, but that comes with the cost of doing so manually and running stale code.

> I am not convinced the first paragraph of your response is a compelling argument.

I said that because if my (or your or some others oduwsdl admins) account gets compromised then the attacker can destroy not just this repository, but many other associated things, even the whole organization without the need of putting a rogue code in a publicly hosted action. This exploit path will be a tiny subset of what would be possible without this. Workflow code runs in a sandbox and operates with a temporary access token that has [permissions scoped to the repository in question only](https://help.github.com/en/actions/configuring-and-managing-workflows/authenticating-with-the-github_token#permissions-for-the-github_token).

> I'm not sure how a message generator api would work for this.

Basically from a 5,000 foot view, I think it's similar to this data generator, but instead of yielding new data, you would be yielding new Messages. This way we would keep messages 1:1 with what's actually being sent, instead of having a separate overload for the "current" data vs the actual data. You can pass in any context variables to the generator that are needed, and the generator returns the next message. This way support for different Arbitration IDs is baked in for "free".

I don't really like the idea of overloading the fact the message data is not immutable. Having 2 fields denoting what the data represents seems non-ideal, since the message no longer represents a CAN message that's being sent/received (but rather, some higher-level protocol notion of a message).

You should be able to hack something together by essentially doing what the default `ThreadBasedCyclicSendTask` is doing as a Proof of Concept, but instead of incrementing the message index, you call the infinite generator. This might result in slightly more boilerplate, but I think it makes things more explicit.

Instead, I think what we probably want is to loosen the restrictions on codegen so that you are able to say `for_sources_types=(SourcesField,), enable_codegen=True` and it will trigger _all_ relevant codegen. This means that a single Protobuf file might generate Java and Python etc. Callers can still use `for_sources_types` to only lazily generate what is relevant, e.g. Python only asks for Python.

The only reason I think I added that restriction when adding codegen was because we wanted codegen to be as lazy as possible. I think the restriction was more than anything meant to assist rule authors to not do the suboptimal thing. Turns out that is causing too many issues.

Ha... I forgot about this. I'm debugging it... I'm going to push one more commit to pretty-print complex NumpyArrays.

The following example:
```python
content_float64 = ak.layout.NumpyArray(
  np.array([0.25, 0.5, 3.5, 4.5, 5.5], dtype=np.float64)
)
content_complex64 = ak.values_astype(content_float64, "complex64", highlevel=False)
array_complex64 = ak.layout.UnmaskedArray(content_complex64)
```
it creates an
```python
<UnmaskedArray>
    <content><NumpyArray format="Zf" shape="5" data="0x 0000803e 00000000 0000003f 00000000 ... 00009040 00000000 0000b040 00000000" at="0x7f9979c44cf0"/></content>
</UnmaskedArray>
```
that is interpreted by `ak.to_list` as an `ak.layout.Content` or `ak.partition.PartitionedArray`. Then `ak.to_list` is iterating over the `bool`, `str`, `bytes`, or `numbers.Number` receiving complex numbers:
```python
to_list 
(0.25+0j)
 is bool, str, bytes, or numbers.Number
to_list 
(0.5+0j)
 is bool, str, bytes, or numbers.Number
to_list 
(3.5+0j)
 is bool, str, bytes, or numbers.Number
to_list 
(4.5+0j)
 is bool, str, bytes, or numbers.Number
to_list 
(5.5+0j)
 is bool, str, bytes, or numbers.Number
```
this results in:
```python
assert ak.to_list(content_complex64) == [0.25, 0.5, 3.5, 4.5, 5.5]
```
while the following works correctly:
```python
assert ak.to_list(ak.nplike.of(array_complex64).asarray(array_complex64)) == [(0.25+0.j), (0.5 +0.j), (3.5 +0.j), (4.5 +0.j), (5.5 +0.j)]
```
because `ak.to_list` is called on  `np.ndarray`:
```diff
to_list 
[0.25+0.j 0.5 +0.j 3.5 +0.j 4.5 +0.j 5.5 +0.j]
np.ndarray
```
Given an example of 
```python
    content_complex128 = ak.values_astype(content_float128, "complex128", highlevel=False)
    array_complex128 = ak.layout.UnmaskedArray(content_complex128)
    assert ak.to_list(content_complex128) == [0.25, 0.5, 3.5, 4.5, 5.5]
```
where
```
to_list 
<NumpyArray format="Zd" shape="5" data="0x 00000000 0000d03f 00000000 00000000 ... 00000000 00001640 00000000 00000000" at="0x7fe6f1e68ca0"/>
ak.layout.NumpyArray
```
`ak.to_list` is fine with `ak.layout.NumpyArray`:
```diff
to_list 
<NumpyArray format="Zd" shape="9" data="0x 9a999999 9999f13f 9a999999 9999b93f ... cdcccccc cccc2340 00000000 00000000" at="0x7f9979c37290"/>
ak.layout.NumpyArray
```

It seems to be the best way (that I've found so far) to keep things DRYish. For example, the release publish job is identical for haskell/rust. What I had using marketplace actions was roughly -

```yaml
  publish:
    if: startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')
    needs:
      - build
    name: Publish github release
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
       - uses: actions/download-artifact@v2
         with:
           path: ./artifacts

       - name: Create Release
         id: create_release
         uses: actions/create-release@v1
         env:
           GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
         with:
           tag_name: ${{ github.event.ref }}
           release_name: ${{ github.event.ref }}
      
       - name: Upload artifacts
         uses: csexton/release-asset-action@v2
         with:
           pattern: |
             artifacts/**/*@(.zip|.tar.gz)
           github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
           release-url: ${{ steps.create_release.outputs.upload_url }}
```

and I wanted to figure out a way to avoid copy-pasting this into `haskell.yaml` and `rust.yaml`. 

There is this concept of [composite steps](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/creating-actions/creating-a-composite-run-steps-action), which in theory let you create new actions from a sequence of steps. The problem is you can't call other actions inside composite actions, you can only use `run` (i.e. script) steps. It's really odd, kind of like a language that lets you define functions, but only if they don't contain other non built-in functions. 

There is actually an issue about it here - https://github.com/actions/runner/issues/646 (what I'm referring to is the lack of `uses` support)

That all being said, not actually that against the copy/paste approach. The js actions are kind of a lot of overhead for not much functionality

The problem I've had in the past was that if calendra wanted to make a change independent of workalendar, it would cause an increment in version numbers. To honor semver, that increment might be a major (1.x) bump. So then calendra is constrained to only commit changes at the same scope as (and coordinated with) workalendar changes or its version numbers must get ahead of workalendar. In short, there's no way to honor semver and keep the numbers in sync. That's why I decided to honor semver and use an otherwise independent versioning scheme.

I use the "Incorporate changes from workalendar vN.N.N" in the changelog to provide a clear and direct association of changes inherited from the upstream. Let's keep that scheme for now.

It's usually not recommended for libraries to load the requirements directly from a requirements.txt file. Both serve different purposes which aren't necessarily compatible to each other.

**install_requires** should offer wide dependency ranges so the library can be installed together with other packages without creating dependency conflicts.
**requirements.txt** should specify a concrete version for each dependency used for testing, usually the latest one.

My suggestion, add the `aiohttp<4.0` dependency here directly and create (only) one `requirements.txt` file which can / should pin specific versions.

Better yet, instead of `setup.py`, define `install_requires` and later `extras_require` in `setup.cfg`.

How long can this loop spin around?

If there is a transient error, a failed unary ACK request could be sent many times in a quick succession here, which is undesirable.

In addition, the `ack()` method could block for a long time while eagerly retrying, effectively blocking the `dispatch_callback()` method and the entire dispatcher. This means, for instance, that any new lease requests added to the dispatch queue will not be processed in time and leased messages could unnecessarily expire.

If we really want to repeatedly make requests here, it should be delegated to a separate thread.

```python
def _extract_eval_level(kwargs):
    """
    Extract `level` from eval or query `kwargs`.

    Remove `level` from the kwargs if it's already there. "level" doesn't
    make sense within the remote execution context, where the remote
    functions have a stack which is different from the stack on the
    driver node that ends in the modin eval call.

    Bump level up one because eval/query are wrapped in the Modin
    logging function wrapper.
    """
    level = kwargs.pop("level", 0) + 1
    return level, kwargs
```
and then use it here like

```python
def query(self, expr, inplace=False, **kwargs):
    # docstring
    level, kwargs = _extract_eval_level(kwargs)
    # do the rest of the stuff

I removed it for multiple reason:
- we actually rely on almost all `Version` properties
- it felt like duplicating the upstream `Version` class which is already wrapping another one
- `Protocol` only provide type hinting, and while I get the "bring your own" point, when contributing I fill less helped by a protocol than a base class
- it was not documented so I felt it was less useful than the base class (I added some docstrings from the upstream `packaging.version`)
- it was not covering the entire scope (I completed it in the last push)
- there is no syntax to explicitely says a class implement a `Protocol`, unless you inherit it (which doesn't bring much value over inheriting directly the `packaging.version.Version`) but you can't inherit both `Version` (which we requires) and `VersionProtocol`
- `Protocol` are not enforced, they only provide type hinting on duck typing leaving developers with only one choice: have a test suite covering all the expected case (which I doubt given none of the existing plugins is covered) because MyPy won't be able to check that it should and implements the protocol (for the same reason, there is no syntax to express it)
- they don't exists in Python 3.7 while being supported by Commitizen

Given I get the "bring your own" point, I restored it in the last push and completed it (missing properties, docstrings...) and found a way to have proper type semantic with it.

> implementing subsetting methods, the seeming reason for existence of IJob

The reason for existence of `IJob` is in the doc block for the class 

> enables these execution APIs to operate on both in memory job
    definitions to be executed in the current process (InMemoryJob) as well as definitions that
    can be reconstructed and executed in a different process (ReconstructableJob)
    
> in cases where that context is required, the type can be limited to ReconstructableJob rather than IJob

This isn't really viable, at least with out further changes, due to how the APIs are layered/shared. Most of the execution APIs in question that operate on `IJob` are hit in both in process and multi process execution. Only once the executor definition is resolved lower in the callstack do you know whether you need reconstruction information. 
    
The subsetting APIs operating on `IJob` / `ReconstructableJob` were to be able to encode the subsetting information on the pointer object that gets passed across process boundaries. I agree this part is messy - and i believe there is inconsistency around callsites expecting to unpack an already subsetted job vs calling subset on the job definition post unpack.

And here are the perf results for (modin_cpus=112), as it's seen the changes mostly affect pure int cases: | | old logic | new logic | |-|-|-| | [test case from the issue](https://github.com/modin-project/modin/issues/4560#issuecomment-1151498644) (524_288, 100),
pure int dataset | 1.48 | 1.69 | | [test case from the issue](https://github.com/modin-project/modin/issues/4560#issuecomment-1151498644) (524_288, 100),
two NaN values in the dataset: | 1.53 | 1.73 | | [test case from the issue](https://github.com/modin-project/modin/issues/4560#issuecomment-1151498644) (524_288, 100),
20% of the dataset values are NaNs: | 2.95 | 3.07 | | real-world dataset from our internal
workload (1_552_210, 214), 77% of the
dataset values are NaNs | 30.6 | 30.58 | | real-world dataset from our internal
workload (1_552_210, 214), but also
calling `.fillna(method="ffill")`
to get rid of NaN values | 4.13 | 5.46 | The reasons why the new approach is inefficient: 1. The map's output now have much more data thus stressing the object store, it used to return (ncols, ncols + 3) shaped dataframe, and now it's (ncols, ncols * 4) which is 3 times heavier. 2. The number of columns to aggregate in [this groupby at the reduce phase](https://github.com/dchigarev/modin/blob/b21fb92e5eba3c72b20b604e3aa3e483a0853ea8/modin/core/storage_formats/pandas/aggregations.py#L331) has tripled as well, thus slowing the reduce phase. 3. [`means` and `std`](https://github.com/dchigarev/modin/blob/b21fb92e5eba3c72b20b604e3aa3e483a0853ea8/modin/core/storage_formats/pandas/aggregations.py#L356-L357) computation is now also slower as they now always operating with square matrices rather than 1d vectors 4. The for-loop building the correlation matrix now have [much more index lookups](https://github.com/dchigarev/modin/blob/b21fb92e5eba3c72b20b604e3aa3e483a0853ea8/modin/core/storage_formats/pandas/aggregations.py#L528-L533) that the [non-nan version of the same loop](https://github.com/dchigarev/modin/blob/b21fb92e5eba3c72b20b604e3aa3e483a0853ea8/modin/core/storage_formats/pandas/aggregations.py#L577-L582).

I think in TF 2 there is honestly not much reason to write proto summaries via TF APIs (as opposed to via APIs that are entirely TF-free, like tensorboardX and the proposed new TB summary API).  The point in TF 1 was to bypass the overhead of building a graph and constructing a session (since the file writing worked without any of that).  But in TF 2, the file writing is op-based anyway; the `write_raw_pb()` API doesn't avoid that.

The purpose I had in adding `write_raw_pb()` was pretty much purely to aid in TF 2 migration for existing TF code that emitted serialized custom summary protos (since otherwise, there isn't an easy way to do an in-graph deconstruction of the protos).  New summary ops should be defined using the `write()` API.

We don't only retry pending pop worker requests when `is_pending_registration` is set to `false` but also when an idle worker is returned. `PushWorker()` handles both case.

If we do it in `OnWorkerStarted()` then we may start more workers than necessary. Imagining a pre-started worker is registered, `OnWorkerStarted()` is called first, if we retry in this function, then we will start a new worker (since the new worker is not pushed yet) but if we retry in `PushWorker` then we can simply use the pre-started worker without starting a new one.

We have the following:
len(nodes): number of nodes at the beginning of _update
len(nodes_to_terminate): number of nodes we are terminating for sure.
The difference len(nodes) - len(nodes_to_terminate) is the number of live nodes if we do not do anything to enforce max_workers.
nodes_we_could_terminate: nodes that got marked with decide_later, ordered by last used time (oldest first).
If we have too many nodes (len(nodes) - len(nodes_to_terminate) > max_workers), we want to terminate some nodes from the tail of nodes_we_could_terminate.

if the configs (resource requests, min_workers) are consistent, we should have num_extra_nodes_to_terminate <= nodes_we_could_terminate, but I would rather not rely on that here.

And you're right...passing a range for `eval_at` causes a failure when serializing the parameters to string to pass them through the C API functions.

```text
  File ".../site-packages/lightgbm/basic.py", line 326, in param_dict_to_str
    raise TypeError(f'Unknown type of parameter:{key}, got:{type(val).__name__}')
TypeError: Unknown type of parameter:eval_at, got:range
```

Given that, I think the hint here should be even stricter than `typing.Sequence`. Since this keyword argument is passed directly through to `params` and there's no other code in LightGBM manipulating its value, I think it can only accept values that are valid for `lightgbm.basic.param_dict_to_str()`.

For `eval_at`, I think that means only a list of ints or tuple of ints is valid. `param_dict_to_str()` supports `list`, `tuple`, and `set`, but `set` isn't appropriate for `eval_at` because sets aren't iterable (e.g. don't have any ordering).

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/3d4e08e1d8be9763f5d55180e3faf1e11eb20787/python-package/lightgbm/basic.py#L323

I just pushed https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/5460/commits/81c234f27f35478ade88857d888190b8554ef47d which:

* sets the hint for `eval_at` to `Union[List[int], Tuple[int]]`
* replaces use of the word "iterable" in the relevant docstrings with "list or tuple of int"

> As I mentioned in our meeting, making a fully zero-copy solution would require modifying OpenThread to be able to send out packets represented directly as a linked buffer chain rather than an `otMessage`. This is an ambitious goal and it probably won't be implemented initially when we release TCP as part of OpenThread.

One possible way of making it happen is to use `otMessage` with just one inner `otMessageBuffer` that is large enough to hold the full TCP segment. We can then effectively use `otMessage` as the `Zero Copy` buffer and achieves real zero copying.

This would require TCP APIs to expose `otMessage` to users, like:

```
otMessage *otTcpNewMessage(void);
```

Then user can prepare data in the `otMessage` directly, which will be eventually sent as the IPv6 datagram. 

BTW, This strategy can also be used by #6036 to achieve `Zero Copy`.

Maybe if there were shared mutable state that had to be manipulated concurrently. Given that the only use of `BSPContext` in this PR is to store the client-provided initialization parameters, however, concurrent access will be to an immutable value that was setup outside of the engine under proper serialization.

In the BSP protocol, any methods calls or notifications sent before the `build/initialize` RPC is made by the client must be discarded. All other threads will send an error back to the client without entering the engine or accessing `BSPContext`. See the first few lines of `_handle_inbound_message`. Only the thread that receives `build/initialize` will update the `BSPContext` with the client-provided setup parameters, and then allow other threads to not error by updating `_initialized`to `True`.

If additional state is added to `BSPContext`, then that particular state might need protection. For example, I intend to add a method to send notifications to the client. That might need locking or not depending on what the protocol library we are using does. But that locking can be handled when the applicable state is added.
